questions.question: What is the most common cause of chronic primary mitral regurgitation?
questions.explanation: Mitral valve prolapse is the most common cause of chronic primary mitral regurgitation in developed countries, due to myxomatous degeneration of the valve leaflet.

questions.question: What auscultatory feature is typical of severe chronic mitral regurgitation?
questions.explanation: Severe chronic mitral regurgitation produces an apical holosystolic murmur that radiates to the axilla, due to regurgitant flow throughout systole.

questions.question: Which echocardiographic finding is characteristic of severe acute mitral regurgitation?
questions.explanation: In severe acute mitral regurgitation, Doppler echocardiography shows regurgitant flow in systole from the left ventricle to the left atrium.

questions.question: What is the surgical treatment of choice in severe primary mitral regurgitation?
questions.explanation: Mitral valve repair is the surgical treatment of choice for severe primary mitral regurgitation, as it preserves ventricular function and avoids anticoagulation.

questions.question: What complication can a patient with untreated chronic mitral regurgitation present?
questions.explanation: Untreated chronic mitral regurgitation can cause pulmonary hypertension secondary to chronically increased pressure in the left atrium and pulmonary venous bed.

questions.question: What pathophysiological mechanism predominates in secondary mitral regurgitation?
questions.explanation: In secondary mitral regurgitation, restriction of the posterior leaflet due to left ventricular dilation or papillary muscle dysfunction prevents adequate coaptation of the leaflets.
